text: This is an 81-year-old female with an adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe.
The dissection is started by mobilizing the inferior pulmonary ligament seen here to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
As can be seen, there are multiple level 9 and level 8 lymph nodes here which are removed and all sent for pathology individually.
Once this is done, the lung is retracted medially to expose the posterior hilum where the level 7 lymphadenectomy will be performed.
Here the posterior pleura is opened, exposing the level 7 lymph nodes.
The robot allows for very meticulous dissection in this space.
and really allows for a complete lymphadenectomy.
Here you can see the carina
with the left main stem bronchus as well as the right main stem bronchus and all the subcranial lymph nodes have been removed.
Next, the apex of the lung is folded down towards the diaphragm and the peritracheal space is exposed.
and all level 4R and 2R lymph nodes are removed.
I do find it beneficial to skeletonize the azagus vein and mobilize all the pleural attachments away from it from the right upper lobe.
Again, this entire area will be freed of all of no tissue.
We'll next start the dissection by identifying the truncus arteriosus, the pulmonary artery to the right upper lobe.
We'll remove this level 10 lymph node to help facilitate the stapler later on in the case.
The lung is then retracted laterally to expose the anterior hilum.
We will proceed with dissecting out the superior pulmonary vein.
In my left hand there is the superior pulmonary vein.
The pulmonary vein will then be transected with a vascular load of the stapler.
We next turn our attention to the right upper lobe pulmonary artery, also known as the truncus arteriosus.
Here a level 10 lymph node is removed to help us better get around the artery.
And once around it, it too will be transected with the vascular load.
Next, we will turn our attention to the fissure.
In this case, the posterior fissure was essentially complete.
We've identified this posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery.
We will go ahead and complete the fissure between the upper lobe and the middle lobe using multiple green loads of the stapler.
Once the upper lobe and middle lobe are separated, we will go ahead and transect this posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery of the vascular lobe.
Lastly, the bronchus to the upper lobe is the only structure remaining.
Again, removing some lymph nodes so we can better accommodate the stapler.
The right upper lobe will then be tented up
to essentially expose the bronchus as seen here.
And then the green load will be used to staple off the right upper lobe bronchus.
An intercostal nerve block is performed.
The specimen is placed in an endo-catch bag and removed.
Chest tube is passed posteriorly and apically, and the lung is then reinflated.
This patient did well and was sent home on postoperative day number one.
